prefix:
Thine eyes I love, and they, as pitying me,
Knowing thy heart torment me with disdain,
Have put on black, and loving mourners be,
target:
Looking with pretty ruth upon my pain.
And truly not the morning sun of heaven
Better becomes the gray cheeks of the east,
Nor that full star that ushers in the even
Doth half that glory to the sober west
As those two mourning eyes become thy face.
O, let it then as well beseem thy heart
To mourn for me, since mourning doth thee grace,
And suit thy pity like in every part.
  Then will I swear beauty herself is black,
  And all they foul that thy complexion lack.
hypothesis:
And call them thence what they are.
  But at last, with so much contempt,
  I was contented, and most honoured.
  My tongue, when I speak, drowns out;
  And yet, after some slurring,
  That which before I say is true,
  To make love to thy dead in verse.
  My tongue then makes love, and so much loves,
  To make my friends, and my enemies be,
  And I can not write more of them than I should write.
  But then, being a child,
  I can write more of them, and thou dost like,
  And canst thou dost more of them than thou dost dost me.
  But then I am happy and happy,
  And then I am an unhappy creature.
   You are happy, my dear, but I am happy.
  So, then, as

prefix:
Beshrew that heart that makes my heart to groan
For that deep wound it gives my friend and me.
Is 't not enough to torture me alone,
target:
But slave to slavery my sweet'st friend must be?
Me from myself thy cruel eye hath taken,
And my next self thou harder hast engrossed;
Of him, myself, and thee I am forsaken,
A torment thrice threefold thus to be crossed.
Prison my heart in thy steel bosom's ward,
But then my friend's heart let my poor heart bail.
Whoe'er keeps me, let my heart be his guard;
Thou canst not then use rigor in my jail.
  And yet thou wilt, for I, being pent in thee,
  Perforce am thine, and all that is in me.
hypothesis:
Or do my broken heart give me room for comfort?
But this heart, too, is a tempest,
Which, of truth, is far more grievous.
  And to say that all these sins which I have done,
  For I am far more eager,
  Than to do my sorrow better by them which I have done.
  And yet, in truth, this world is a happy one,
  For my love is a far greater friend than my enemies.
  If love, so easily let alone love,
  I have no fear to love, nor can I see it;
  And what of love is so cruel that such love can escape me?
  I have thought that love should be found in all the world.
  But this is not true.  I do love all, and the world is so far from love,
  So that I can feel no love that can stay at home.

prefix:
So, now I have confessed that he is thine
And I myself am mortgaged to thy will,
Myself I'll forfeit, so that other mine
target:
Thou wilt restore to be my comfort still.
But thou wilt not, nor he will not be free,
For thou art covetous, and he is kind;
He learned but surety-like to write for me
Under that bond that him as fast doth bind.
The statute of thy beauty thou wilt take,
Thou usurer that put'st forth all to use,
And sue a friend came debtor for my sake;
So him I lose through my unkind abuse.
  Him have I lost; thou hast both him and me.
  He pays the whole, and yet am I not free.
hypothesis:
may live
And play my part in my deeds.
Now, let me give thee thy fault,
Thou alone and I alone will hold thee,
And let no such evil help me,
Nor may it be thy favor to make me an evil.
  When thou art left, so shall I be.
  But I am truly thy man,
  And that which should give thee this hope,
   I shall make thee a man of my own.
  So, if thou art not well, thou art the work of mine eye.
  And if thou art not well, I shall see thee alive.
  But it is well that I will live, and not die.
  Then I will write to thee in praise;
  Then, then, I will make thee a man.
  But in spite of thee I will not live.
  But if thou art well, thou art my friend.
  And if

prefix:
Whoever hath her wish, thou hast thy will,
And will to boot, and will in overplus.
More than enough am I that vex thee still,
target:
To thy sweet will making addition thus.
Wilt thou, whose will is large and spacious,
Not once vouchsafe to hide my will in thine?
Shall will in others seem right gracious,
And in my will no fair acceptance shine?
The sea, all water, yet receives rain still,
And in abundance addeth to his store;
So thou, being rich in will, add to thy will
One will of mine to make thy large will more.
  Let no unkind, no fair beseechers kill.
  Think all but one, and me in that one will.
hypothesis:
For that thou hast seen it;
For that it is now thy duty to my fortune to make
That the sight which thou art able to see
Thy seat be the best in the world;
To think of this in my eyes, and of the same
In thy thoughts, and of the same.
  But thou, being called of what thou art,
  I am not so much above thy thoughts as thou
  Where thy thoughts are.  If I should make thee think
  That this thy thoughts are so sweet, that I must not think thee
  Which I am, you will be happy, and I will not think thee.
  For I am of thee alone, and I do not need thee
  To speak, and thou alone can make my thought.
   But thou, being called of what thou art,
  I am not so much above thy thoughts as thou.
  Where thy thoughts are, I cannot

prefix:
If thy soul check thee that I come so near,
Swear to thy blind soul that I was thy will,
And will, thy soul knows, is admitted there.
target:
Thus far for love my love-suit, sweet, fulfill.
Will will fulfill the treasure of thy love,
Ay, fill it full with wills, and my will one.
In things of great receipt with ease we prove
Among a number one is reckoned none.
Then in the number let me pass untold,
Though in thy store's account I one must be.
For nothing hold me, so it please thee hold
That nothing me, a something, sweet, to thee.
  Make but my name thy love, and love that still,
  And then thou lovest me, for my name is Will.
hypothesis:
If thou art a fair mortal,
Thou willest me meet and pay the fee,
And yet thy self will not be the free hand.
  But if thou art fair and fair,
  Or fair, in fair weather, fair,
  Then are you fair to me, fair, fair.
  Then I shall not say,
  " If thou art fair, my soul must be with thee."
  I am the fair or fair, for thee,
  Or fair, in fair weather, fair, fair.
  I am the fair or fair, and my soul should be free.
 
  If thou art fair and fair, I may make thee fair.
  But if thou art fair, my soul must not be with thee.
  I shall not say, " If thou art fair, my soul must not be with thee."
  If thou art fair, my soul must not be with thee.

prefix:
Thou blind fool, Love, what dost thou to mine eyes
That they behold and see not what they see?
They know what beauty is, see where it lies,
target:
Yet what the best is take the worst to be.
If eyes, corrupt by overpartial looks,
Be anchored in the bay where all men ride,
Why of eyes' falsehood hast thou forged hooks,
Whereto the judgment of my heart is tied?
Why should my heart think that a several plot
Which my heart knows the wide world's common place?
Or mine eyes, seeing this, say this is not,
To put fair truth upon so foul a face?
  In things right true my heart and eyes have erred,
  And to this false plague are they now transferred.
hypothesis:
And they see that beauty is not of all things,
If, as thou art, that beauty be with thyself,
Or, that beauty not which thou art,
That beauty be with thyself, and not with thyself.
  And then do not thou see what thou see
  And do not speak of the beauty that thou art;
  For the eyes of thy sight which thou dost see,
  And that eye which thou see is not with thee.
  But that eye which thou see is not of thyself.
  And then do thou perceive what thou see,
  And do not speak of the beauty that thou see,
  For it is not thy sight that thou see,
  But this eyes of thy sight which thou see,
  That eye which thou see perceives thyself,
  And then do not speak of the beauty which thou see.

prefix:
When my love swears that she is made of truth
I do believe her though I know she lies,
That she might think me some untutored youth,
target:
Unlearned in the world's false subtleties.
Thus vainly thinking that she thinks me young,
Although she knows my days are past the best,
Simply I credit her false-speaking tongue;
On both sides thus is simple truth suppressed.
But wherefore says she not she is unjust?
And wherefore say not I that I am old?
O, love's best habit is in seeming trust,
And age in love loves not to have years told.
  Therefore I lie with her and she with me,
  And in our faults by lies we flattered be.
hypothesis:
Nor dare tell me that I may be deceived,
That I will not be sorry, nor do I believe her.
But this should not be too much, for she was born of no need;
The purpose of the birth was to bear witness
To me, my heart, and in my body,
To the proud and virtuous nature of my self.
So I am called a scoundrel, and she was
A foolish young woman's mistress, in my own breast.
  And if I thought her fair,
  And if she were fair, I should be blameless,
  As a plain maiden, like her mother.
  And what her name is I cannot see,
  But you must think I think her, that she is fair,
  That she is fair, that I see her fair,
  And that I see her fair, that I may see her.
  Then I can no longer be fair,

target:
Use power with power, and slay me not by art.
Tell me thou lov'st elsewhere; but in my sight,
Dear heart, forbear to glance thine eye aside.
What need'st thou wound with cunning when thy might
Is more than my o'erpressed defense can bide?
Let me excuse thee: ah, my love well knows
Her pretty looks have been mine enemies;
And therefore from my face she turns my foes,
That they elsewhere might dart their injuries.
  Yet do not so; but since I am near slain,
  Kill me outright with looks, and rid my pain.

prefix:
Be wise as thou art cruel; do not press
My tongue-tied patience with too much disdain,
Lest sorrow lend me words, and words express
target:
The manner of my pity-wanting pain.
If I might teach thee wit, better it were,
Though not to love, yet, love, to tell me so,
As testy sick men, when their deaths be near,
No news but health from their physicians know.
For if I should despair, I should grow mad,
And in my madness might speak ill of thee.
Now this ill-wresting world is grown so bad,
Mad slanderers by mad ears believed be.
  That I may not be so, nor thou belied,
  Bear thine eyes straight, though thy proud heart go wide.
hypothesis:
Darling's love to that which I have endured.
For love's love is ever so active
As to be by the love of men's sweet form.
And therefore, knowing no shame in love,
Of love's face or beauty in truth,
It was for thy sake to shame me,
Therefore I now love thee as thy beauty.
  I, therefore, too, am the eyes of the world,
  And therefore my love doth make no difference.
  But though love's love be ever so active
  And how do I love, knowing no shame,
  But from that day on, my love shall make no difference.
  Then, my love shall be never less than thy beauty,
  And I shall be thy sweet, my beauty's sweet.
  As well, then, I shall be yours, but mine love shall no longer be.
  If you love me, I will be your only sweet

target:
Who in despite of view is pleased to dote.
Nor are mine ears with thy tongue's tune delighted,
Nor tender feeling to base touches prone,
Nor taste, nor smell, desire to be invited
To any sensual feast with thee alone.
But my five wits nor my five senses can
Dissuade one foolish heart from serving thee,
Who leaves unswayed the likeness of a man,
Thy proud heart's slave and vassal wretch to be.
  Only my plague thus far I count my gain,
  That she that makes me sin awards me pain.
hypothesis:
And what I do not love, I love thee
For every thief's shame, and every bounty that lives.
  But if I love thee with my eye,
  I may hate thee with my tongue,
  And the stain of thy crime may shine forth.
  To no man I am able to persuade,
  To some I am even without hope,
  For if I love thee with mine eyes,
  To others I do nothing but forgive.
  And when thou art my friend, I forgive thee
  To make thee my friend; for it is not my purpose to say;
  For it is thy shame that my eyes will never see.
   Yet thou art my friend's friend; I am not thy friend's wife.
  But if I love thee with my tongue,  And yet thou dost love me with mine eyes,
  Then thy thoughts shall not show me,
  And thou art my

prefix:
Love is my sin, and thy dear virtue hate,
Hate of my sin, grounded on sinful loving.
O, but with mine compare thou thine own state,
target:
And thou shalt find it merits not reproving.
Or if it do, not from those lips of thine,
That have profaned their scarlet ornaments
And sealed false bonds of love as oft as mine,
Robbed others' beds' revenues of their rents.
Be it lawful I love thee as thou lov'st those
Whom thine eyes woo as mine importune thee;
Root pity in thy heart, that, when it grows,
Thy pity may deserve to pitied be.
  If thou dost seek to have what thou dost hide,
  By self-example mayst thou be denied.
hypothesis:
That thou, my love, thy art, that thy love should by itself be happy.
Thy love in thee is ever so strong as mine own,
Which I might with thy hand live.
But the poor my love might live,
And be such in thee as is willing.
  For this love of mine, thou art love, that thy own love should love.
  Therefore thou art my love, and not mine own,
  For I should not be like thee in the love of my own.
  Therefore thou art mine; and I love thee
  My love is not mine own, but thy love,  For my love is worth me.
  I myself love thee in thee, but this love is thy love,
  For my love is worth mine.  Thus, I love thee, and not thou,
  But with thee I love thy love,  For I love thee all.
  I myself love thee

prefix:
Lo, as a careful huswife runs to catch
One of her feathered creatures broke away,
Sets down her babe, and makes all swift dispatch
target:
In pursuit of the thing she would have stay,
Whilst her neglected child holds her in chase,
Cries to catch her whose busy care is bent
To follow that which flies before her face,
Not prizing her poor infant's discontent;
So runn'st thou after that which flies from thee,
Whilst I, thy babe, chase thee afar behind.
But if thou catch thy hope, turn back to me
And play the mother's part: kiss me, be kind.
  So will I pray that thou mayst have thy will,
  If thou turn back and my loud crying still.
hypothesis:
To her beloved bosom, and gives her every love
For those sweet-tamed who no longer call.
When she's in love, she's slow to lose;
Then she's a double-breasted, and fast with two hearts,
And holds her beloved bosom in her chest;
Then she's an imposter, and hoary to receive.
  But this time, it's the way she's going;
  As she wails on, she keeps straight,
  So she's the one to take charge;
  Which is true, as they say, by a woe,
  If she cannot see me.
  But I believe that my mother hath taken her back.
  I love her love, and love her heart as much as myself.
  To be the slowest and most perfect flower,
  I love the world more than I love myself.
